title: Spring simmer pot
subtitle: A stovetop fragrance with lemon, rosemary, and vanilla
excerpt: A stovetop simmer pot made from lemon slices, fresh rosemary, cinnamon stick, and vanilla pod, simmered in water to fill the kitchen with a clean, fresh-spring scent. The ingredients are not consumed; they are simmered for fragrance only. Add water as needed. Discard after one day.
sourceNotes: Stovetop simmer pot as a traditional fragrance method, widely documented in home fragrance literature. Ingredient combination adapted from common spring-scent combinations in food and fragrance sources.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 simmer pot session (2 to 4 hours of fragrance)
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make fresh each time. The ingredients can be sliced and assembled the day before and stored in the fridge until ready to simmer.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Discard after 24 hours. The simmered ingredients will become mouldy if kept longer. Do not consume the contents.

glossaryTerms.definition: A pot of water with aromatic ingredients held at a low simmer on the stovetop. The steam carries volatile aromatic compounds into the air of the kitchen. Unlike a reed diffuser or candle, the scent is produced only while the pot is on the heat and stops immediately when the heat is turned off. It is a short-use, single-session fragrance method.

The main safety concern with a simmer pot is allowing the water to evaporate completely. Once the pot is dry, the remaining organic material will scorch and burn. Check the water level every 30 minutes and add more as needed. Never leave a simmer pot unattended.

content.text: is the simplest room fragrance method: aromatic ingredients are placed in a pan of water and held at a gentle simmer on the stovetop. The steam releases the volatile compounds into the kitchen air. This spring version uses lemon for brightness, fresh rosemary for the green-herbal note, cinnamon for warmth, and vanilla pod for depth. The result is cleaner and fresher than the typical autumn-spice simmer pot combination.
The single most important safety point:
never let the pot boil dry
. Check the water level every 30 minutes and top up as needed. Never leave the pot unattended on the heat. Do not consume the simmered ingredients.

items.prepNote: Unwaxed if available. Slice thinly. The peel carries the limonene compounds that give the citrus top note.
Fresh rosemary sprigs. Lightly bruise the leaves before adding to release the volatile oils.
One whole cinnamon stick. Adds warmth that grounds the citrus and rosemary.
Split lengthways to release the aromatic compounds. A spent vanilla pod used for cooking works as well as a fresh one for fragrance purposes.

content.text: Fill the pan with 1 litre of water. Add the lemon slices, bruised rosemary sprigs, cinnamon stick, and split vanilla pod. Bring to a simmer over medium heat, then reduce to the lowest possible simmer. The water should steam but not boil actively.

Check the pan every 30 minutes. Add water to keep the level at least 2 cm above the ingredients. After 2 to 4 hours, the fragrance compounds in the ingredients will be largely exhausted; the scent will have faded from the steam. Turn off the heat and discard the contents.

Discard all the simmered ingredients at the end of the session. Do not store leftover simmered water; it will become cloudy and may grow mould within hours at room temperature. The cinnamon stick can be rinsed and dried for a second use. The lemon and rosemary are single-use.
